---
title: Cross-Sectional Analysis
---

`q.cross_section` owns characteristics calculated across a universe,
including ranks, neutralization, and relative rating models such as Elo.

## Universe transforms

`q.cross_section.rank`, `percentile_rank`, and `zscore` accept one universe
snapshot as a `Series` or a time-by-asset panel as a `DataFrame`. DataFrames
operate across columns by default and preserve their index, columns, and
missing values.

```python
ranked = q.cross_section.percentile_rank(signals)
standardized = q.cross_section.zscore(signals)
```

`q.cross_section.neutralize` removes aligned numeric or categorical exposures
from one snapshot with least squares. Optional positive weights select weighted
least squares. Rows with missing values remain missing rather than being
silently imputed.

```python
residual_signal = q.cross_section.neutralize(
	signal,
	exposures[["beta", "sector"]],
	weights=market_cap,
)
```

`q.cross_section.relative_strength(prices, lookback=21)` calculates trailing
returns from a time-by-asset price matrix and percentile-ranks assets at every
timestamp. This differs from `q.indicator.relative_strength`, which compares
one instrument's price path with an explicit benchmark.

## Group-weighted returns

`q.cross_section.group_weighted_return` aggregates long-form asset returns by
date and a required group column, which defaults to `sectorid`. It uses a
`return` column when available, otherwise calculates simple returns from
`close`. Other price or return columns must be supplied with an explicit
`field_type`.

Equal, market-cap, volume, inverse-volatility, and custom-column weights are
supported. Non-equal weights are lagged by one symbol observation by default
so the return ending at time $t$ does not use information first observed at
$t$. Missing returns or non-positive weights are excluded, and valid weights
are renormalized within each date and group.

The result contains one row per date and group with `weighted_return`,
`asset_count`, and the raw `weight_sum`. A tutorial will be added when a
multi-symbol dataset with real sector classifications is available.

## Elo ratings

`q.cross_section.compute_elo` compares each symbol with nearby-rated symbols in the
same sector on each date. Call it once with the complete long-form universe;
do not group by symbol before calling it.

The input requires `date`, `symbol`, `close`, and `sectorid`. The sector column
is not optional because matches are constrained to peers in the same sector.
Missing sector values are placed in one `"UnknownSector"` bucket.

```python
rated = q.cross_section.compute_elo(
    daily_prices,  # date, symbol, close, sectorid
    daily_vol_window=20,
    matches_per_stock=1,
    initial_elo=1500,
    rf_data=daily_risk_free_rates,  # optional: date, rate
)
```

The result includes prepared return/volatility columns, the current `elo`, the
day's `elo_change`, and cumulative `matches_played`. If `rf_data` is supplied
and `use_excess_returns=True`, matches use log return minus the aligned daily
risk-free rate; otherwise they use log returns.

This is a migration of the legacy model, not a claim that every modeling choice
has been settled. See the [cross-section roadmap](roadmap.qmd) for the known match
scheduling, K-factor, sample-data, and missing-price issues.

A factor or group return measures cross-sectional characteristics; portfolio
selection and position sizing belong in `q.signal` and `q.portfolio`.
